how i can study for a big exam in 2 days

You’ve got this. In two days, focused, high-yield prep plus active practice can make a big difference. Here’s a practical, no-fluss plan you can use right away.

What to do first
- Get the basics: print or open your syllabus, can you access past papers or example questions, and gather any formula sheets or key notes.
- Identify high-yield topics: scan past papers or your notes and pick 3–5 topics that tend to show up or that you feel less confident about.
- Create a simple 1-page cheat sheet: 1 page of formulas, key concepts, common question types—this reinforces memory and gives you quick reviews.

Two-day study plan (about 5 hours per day)
Day 1
- Block 1 (60 min): Quick topic scan + deep dive into Topic A
  - Review core concepts in 1 topic (use notes, a diagram, or a concept map).
- Break (10–15 min)
- Block 2 (60 min): Practice Topic A
  - Do 6–10 short problems or a few past questions. Focus on applying the concepts.
- Break (10–15 min)
- Block 3 (60 min): Topic B
  - Same approach as Topic A (brief review + targeted practice).
- Break (10–15 min)
- Block 4 (60 min): Topic B practice
  - 6–10 problems or a mini past-paper set.
- Break (10–15 min)
- Block 5 (40–50 min): Review + cheat sheet
  - Note mistakes, fill gaps, tighten your one-page sheet.

Day 2
- Block 1 (60 min): Timed past-paper or mixed questions (Section 1)
  - Do 1 timed block similar to exam conditions if you can.
- Break (10–15 min)
- Block 2 (60 min): Review answers and fix gaps
  - Read solutions, note where you went wrong, re-do tricky questions.
- Break (10–15 min)
- Block 3 (60 min): Timed second pass
  - Another block of past questions or a different topic set, under timed conditions.
- Break (10–15 min)
- Block 4 (40–50 min): Final review of error log + cheat sheet
  - Tighten notes on the most common mistakes.
- Break (10 min)
- Block 5 (40–50 min): Full quick mock exam or rapid-fire recap
  - Do a short practice set or run through your cheat sheet aloud; simulate exam stress to build familiarity, then wind down.

Key study techniques to use now
- Active recall: after you review a topic, close your notes and summarize it from memory.
- Practice, not just reread: aim to solve problems or answer questions rather than passively reading.
- Spaced-ish review: at the end of each block, jot a couple of quick questions you still find tricky and target them tomorrow.
- Explain it simply: use the Feynman technique—explain the concept to yourself in plain language or pretend you’re teaching someone else.
- Mistake log: every time you get something wrong, write down what tripped you and how to fix it.

Exam-day mindset tips
- Read instructions first, skim the whole paper, and mark easy questions to secure quick marks.
- Allocate time by section and keep a rough pace; don’t get stuck on one hard problem.
- If you’re stuck, move on and come back later with fresh eyes.
- Stay calm: breathe, drink water, and trust your preparation.

What I can tailor for you
- Tell me your subject and any topics you’re worried about.
- Do you have past papers or a syllabus? If yes, share the topics or questions you’ve seen most often.
- I can adjust the two-day plan to fit your exact topics and the exam format.

You’re capable of making the most of these 2 days. Ready to share your subject and any materials you have so I tailor the plan even more precisely?

Hi adaaa! I’m doing great—thanks for asking. How are you doing? Love your aim to prepare for exams. Here’s a practical, no-fluzz plan for your 5 hours today to keep you moving steadily.

Today’s 5-hour plan (with short breaks between blocks)
- Block 1 (50 min) Subject 1: Focus on 1–2 key topics for the June 15 exam. Create a concise 5-bullet summary for each topic and solve 3 representative practice problems. Quick check: what’s your top weak spot in Subject 1 right now?
- Break (10 min)
- Block 2 (50 min) Logic: Tackle hard topics. Do 8–12 problems, and jot down the main mistakes you’re making. Create a short note for each mistake so you don’t repeat it.
- Break (10 min)
- Block 3 (50 min) Subject 1: Targeted practice on your identified weak area. Do 15–20 problems, review solutions, and annotate where you went wrong.
- Break (10 min)
- Block 4 (50 min) Logic: Deep-dive review. Revisit core concepts, sketch a simple formula/map, and summarize one “cheat sheet” page you can flip through later.
- Break (10 min)
- Block 5 (50 min) Subject 1: Final wrap-up. Do a quick self-test (5–10 problems) and create 6 flashcards or a one-page recap of today’s learning. End with a concrete plan for tomorrow (which topics to tackle first).

Tips to stay on track
- Start clearly with one concrete goal per block (e.g., “finish Topic A summary and 3 problems”).
- If you’re ever stuck, switch to a quick “explain it to a peer” style note for 2 minutes; that helps solidify understanding.
- End with a 2-minute read of your notes to lock in what you learned.

If you want, tell me the exact topics you’re covering in Subject 1 and the hardest concepts in Logic. I can tailor today’s blocks precisely to those items. You’ve got this—one focused day at a time, and you’ll see progress.

Yes—I can help with a lot more than just typing. As your study coach, I can:

- Create a personalized 5-hour/day study plan aligned to your deadlines (Subject 1 exam on 2026-06-15; Logic exam on 2026-07-14).

- Track your progress, adjust the plan weekly, and set reminders.
- Simulate full-length practice tests and review results to improve.

You’ve got this, Adama. Let’s keep it super simple and still powerful. Here’s an easy, repeatable plan that fits your 5 hours a day and your exam on 2026-06-15.

Plan at a glance
- Duration: about 7 weeks, with a built-in quick wrap-up week before the exam.
- Daily rhythm (5 hours): 
  - 15 min: quick recap of yesterday
  - 90 min: core concept study (theory, notes, short readings)
  - 90 min: practical task (case, design thinking activity, mini-prototype)
  - 60 min: exam-style practice question or short quiz
  - 60 min: review + plan for next day
- Weekly focus:
  - Week 1: Foundations – stages of design thinking, empathy, reframing problems
  - Week 2: Ideation – generating ideas, selecting concepts
  - Week 3: Prototyping – quick prototypes, low-fidelity tests
  - Week 4: Testing & case studies – feedback, real-world examples
  - Week 5: Exam practice – memory aids, structured answers
  - Week 6: Full practice exams – targeted review of weak areas
  - Week 7: Final polish + confidence boost (buffer week)
- Weekly check-in: quick 10-minute review on Friday to adjust next week if something isn’t clicking.

Starter week outline (example you can copy each week)
- Monday: 
  - 15 min recap
  - 90 min concept study (new topic or chapter)
  - 90 min practical task (case or simple design activity)
  - 60 min practice question
  - 60 min review/plan
- Tuesday–Thursday: Repeat the same blocks with a fresh topic or case each day
- Friday: 15 min recap, 90 min review of week notes, 90 min light practice or a low-stakes case, 60 min summary + plan for next week
- Weekend (optional): 1–2 hours optional catch-up or reflection

Tips to keep it easy and sustainable
- Use one central space: a single notebook or digital doc for quick notes, diagrams, and your “why this matters” statements.
- Keep tasks small and tangible: one mini-prototype, one case study, one exam-style answer.
- If energy dips, swap a 90-min block for two 45-min blocks with a 5–10 min break.
- Track progress with a simple checkbox per day or a one-line journal: “Key takeaway from today?” “One question for tomorrow.”
- If you miss a day, don’t overcram—just move on to the next day’s plan. Consistency beats perfection.

Next steps
- Want, I can turn this into a printable 7-week calendar with dates and daily tasks tailored to היום (today) and your actual class schedule.
- If you share any particular topics you find tricky, I’ll adjust Week 1 or Week 2 to emphasize them.

You’re building a solid, exam-ready way of thinking. Stay steady, and you’ll get there. If you’d like, tell me your preferred start date and any fixed days off, and I’ll customize the calendar for you.
